name: Akh
description: The Akh, in ancient Egyptian beliefs, represents the transfigured soul of a deceased individual. It is a crucial aspect of the complex Egyptian understanding of the afterlife, emerging after the successful completion of trials in the Duat (underworld). Unlike the physical body (khat) or the life force (ka), the Akh is a more ethereal and potent form of existence. It is often depicted as a crested ibis bird, symbolizing its ability to travel freely between the world of the living and the realm of the gods. The Akh possessed magical powers and could intercede in the affairs of the living, either helping or hindering them. Achieving Akhu, or becoming an Akh, was the ultimate goal of funerary rituals, ensuring the deceased's continued existence and influence in the cosmos. Its existence depended on the preservation of the body and the continued offerings and prayers from the living.
subtitle: The transfigured soul of the deceased in Egyptian mythology
appearance: Often depicted as a crested ibis bird, but can also manifest as a luminous or shining being. Retains some semblance of the deceased's personality and appearance.
habitat: Exists primarily in the Duat (underworld) and can traverse between the world of the living and the celestial realm.
behavior: The behavior of an Akh is dependent on the life lived and the treatment received after death. They can be benevolent, offering guidance and protection, or malevolent, causing misfortune if neglected or angered. They require sustenance in the form of offerings and prayers from the living to maintain their existence and power.

keyMyths.title: The Weighing of the Heart Ceremony
keyMyths.description: The Akh's formation is contingent on the successful navigation of the Duat and the Weighing of the Heart ceremony. If the heart is lighter than the feather of Ma'at (truth), the deceased is granted passage to the afterlife and becomes an Akh. Failure results in annihilation by Ammit, the Devourer of Souls. This myth highlights the importance of moral conduct in life to achieve eternal existence.

keyMyths.title: The Role of Offerings and Prayers
keyMyths.description: The Akh requires sustenance in the form of offerings and prayers from the living. These rituals provide the Akh with the energy needed to maintain its existence and influence. Neglect of the deceased can lead to the Akh becoming restless and potentially vengeful, highlighting the importance of ancestor veneration in ancient Egyptian society. The Pyramid Texts and Coffin Texts detail these practices.

keyMyths.title: The Akh's Influence on the Living
keyMyths.description: Akhs could intervene in the affairs of the living, offering guidance, protection, or even causing misfortune. They were seen as powerful intermediaries between the gods and humanity. Dreams and visions were often attributed to the influence of Akhs, highlighting their continued presence and impact on the world. Stories of benevolent and malevolent Akhs are common in Egyptian folklore.

sources.author: Assmann, Jan
sources.work: Death and Salvation in Ancient Egypt
sources.description: Provides a comprehensive overview of Egyptian funerary beliefs and the role of the Akh in the afterlife.
sources.text: Provides a comprehensive overview of Egyptian funerary beliefs and the role of the Akh in the afterlife.

sources.author: Faulkner, Raymond O.
sources.work: The Ancient Egyptian Book of the Dead
sources.description: A translation and commentary on the Book of the Dead, detailing the journey of the deceased through the Duat and the attainment of Akhu.
sources.text: A translation and commentary on the Book of the Dead, detailing the journey of the deceased through the Duat and the attainment of Akhu.

sources.author: Pinch, Geraldine

sources.description: A general guide to Egyptian mythology, including information on the Akh and its significance.
sources.text: A general guide to Egyptian mythology, including information on the Akh and its significance.
